title: Why Won't You Apologize? Healing Big Betrayals and Everyday Hurts
author: Harriet Lerner

chapters.title: The apology gap
chapters.text:
Some people apologize readily — even for events outside their control. "I'm sorry you argued with your parents" or "I'm sorry traffic made them miss dinner" can express genuine empathy and connection.

In contrast, others treat apology like profanity and avoid it entirely.

Harriet Lerner once discovered an organization printing an outdated photo for her lecture despite repeated requests and a replacement image. Instead of apologizing, a representative told her to go easier on herself about her appearance.

People who apologize but receive no forgiveness often recast themselves as victims.

Improving how we repair conflict starts with clarifying responsibility and finding language that honors both positions and feelings. It's time to understand the responsibilities of conflicts and find the right words to explain your position and share your feelings.

chapters.title: Apologies that make things worse
chapters.text:
Conflict repair requires hearing each other — and knowing which "sorry" deepens the wound. So, what do bad apologies sound like?

• The one that includes a "but"
A but cancels remorse and pivots to justification. Explanations belong only after genuine accountability.

• "I'm sorry that it hurt you"
This reframes the offender's negligence as the victim's oversensitivity. Recall Lerner's lecture photo: the manager cited busyness, then "apologized" for her sensitivity about age and appearance.

• Anger in the absence of forgiveness
Some expect instant amnesty after a rote sorry. When forgiveness is withheld, they become the injured party. Real apologies are conscious and leave room for the hurt person to process and forgive.

• "Look what you made me do"
Lerner's son once refused to return a toy; another child banged his head on the floor. The child's mother forced an apology for an injury her son did not cause. We cannot own each other's choices.

• Obsessive apology
People who cannot accept rejection may repeat sorry past the point of usefulness. When someone declines an apology, respect the boundary.

"No" is a complete sentence. It should be enough to stop any interactions you don't like.

chapters.title: Criticism, conflict, and repair
chapters.text:
Evolution wired humans for self-defense — a reflex that fires in mortal danger and mundane arguments alike. When we begin to blame another person, the other party prepares explanations and accusations in response.

An apology is more difficult to offer when we do have something to apologize for and we regret our earlier behavior. ~ Harriet Lerner, PhD

Apologizing is hardest under criticism, yet those moments often decide whether a relationship survives or deepens.

We apologize sincerely when the bond matters more than the issue.

Partners, friends, and relatives can rewrite family scripts. Even households that treated emotional talk as weakness can adopt evening conversations about what is on their minds and hearts, shifting focus from "you hurt me" to "I feel."

Still, one partner may list 117 grievances while the other adds 200. The next minutes demand slow breathing and listening aimed at understanding, not scorekeeping. Reconnect without fresh accusations or demands for instant resolution. Offer apology only when reciprocity returns.

The process may take longer and require additional effort, as you may not be ready to process everything immediately. Open questions and genuine curiosity rebuild bridges.

chapters.title: Why cultures clash over sorry
chapters.text:
Lerner's nephew once dated a Korean woman who never apologized — not to him, her parents, or friends. She assumed closeness guaranteed forgiveness, making sorry unnecessary.

Every relationship blends two micro-cultures, even between neighbors raised on the same street. Values, thinking styles, and attitudes to conflicts differ.

Perfectionists may equate apology with admitting fatal flaw, fearing lost status — often imagined rather than real.

For chronic non-apologizers, saying or accepting sorry can feel manipulative.

Guilt, when earned, motivates repair. Shame — the sense that the self is defective — pushes people toward over-apologizing for things they did not cause or toward blaming others to avoid admitting fault.

Did you know? Gender stereotypes from childhood negatively affect the ability to apologize. For example, a common stereotype for boys is "Be a man!" This applies to the prohibition of crying or appearing weak and an inability to admit mistakes and apologize for them. As for girls, the sexist "Be a lady!" forces them to accept almost all stereotypical apologies not to seem rude and cold.

chapters.title: Forgiveness is not the only path
chapters.text:
Moving from apologizing well to deciding whether to forgive raises separate questions.

Social media floods us with quotes insisting forgiveness liberates the forgiver and signals strength.

Some wounds are too deep for either forgiveness or forgetting. Telling victims they must forgive to heal can retraumatize. They may need understanding and restored peace instead.

The desire to forgive an offender and to free yourself from anger or pain are two different goals.

Letting go offers an alternative. It acknowledges harm without requiring affection for the person who caused it — and without clutching pain forever.

Release often begins when past hurt blocks present joy. Therapy can restore agency without forgiving an abuser.

Emotions rarely arrive one at a time.

Lerner's son once asked whether she would still love him if he seriously harmed family members and went to prison. She answered honestly: she might hate him at times and refuse forgiveness, yet still love him, communicate with him, and visit him in prison.

Thoughts about forgiveness after conflict or trauma are only the tip of the iceberg. Track the full emotional spectrum. When ready, you can begin letting feelings move through you.

chapters.title: Practical steps toward letting go
chapters.text:
Psychological insight is easier to describe than to live. How do you stop replaying harm when forgiveness and forgetting both feel impossible?

Start by sorting people by how much their opinions should matter. A colleague's offhand comment about your appearance may reflect their mood, not your worth.

Don't take things quite so personally; unhappiness or insecurity can make people say stupid things. ~ Harriet Lerner, PhD

Avoid inventing rationalizations for outrageous behavior or obsessing over what you could have done differently — that path invites victimhood.

When we want to identify the motives for a person's actions, we often take steps to normalize them.

Do not wonder whether an abuser feels remorse without a sincere apology. Focus inward instead of mind-reading.

Reaching out to vent rarely produces guilt in someone who already knows the damage and feels no shame. No one can force guilt on others or artificially induce a desire to apologize.

Finally, honor the real impact of betrayal or ended relationships. Strength includes feeling pain and eventually releasing it.

Not every story ends with repentant villains and gracious victims. Find the courage to talk about your experience, how it changed you, and what it taught you.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Although there are at least two sides to every story, each person can only describe and take care of their own experiences. Name feelings accurately, accept apologies that ring true, and ignore provocations designed to bait you. Finally, give yourself time to let go without minimizing what occurred.

Try this

• Watch favorite films and study conflict scenes. How do characters resolve them? Would you emulate or reject their choices?
• Which of the five apology pitfalls have you faced? Practice responding calmly to pseudo-apologies.
• Consider whether forgetting, forgiving, or letting go has helped after major quarrels — and whether the relationship survived.

Read it till the end?
